roles.name: Poet Laureate
roles.description: Crowned voice of the canon — designs the over-arching narrative architecture and watches over its integrity.

roles.name: Editor-in-Chief
roles.description: Marshals the writers' room, holds the deadline, and answers to the publisher.

roles.name: Keeper of the Stacks
roles.description: Steward of the library — catalogues, layouts, fields and permission sets.

roles.name: Master Typesetter
roles.description: Forges Apex, LWC, and platform features at the press.

roles.name: Senior Lecturer in Letters
roles.description: Chair of the faculty — sets standards and guides the senior writers.

roles.name: Reader of Manuscripts
roles.description: Patrols the borders of customer requirements with a practiced editorial eye.

roles.name: Junior Reader
roles.description: Carries discovery and configuration across the canon under the Reader's eye.

roles.name: Translator-in-Residence
roles.description: Architect of the great spans between Salesforce and the realms beyond — translates one tongue to another.

roles.name: Assistant Translator
roles.description: Lays the stones of API, middleware, and event flows under the Translator's plan.

roles.name: Apprentice Translator
roles.description: Learns the craft of integration — webhooks, batch, callouts and the intricate patterns of error handling.

roles.name: Proofreader
roles.description: Reads the galleys with a sharp pen — finds the errata in the build before it reaches the press.

roles.name: Publisher
roles.description: Lifts the imprint and the trumpet for the catalogue — leads all marketing craft.

roles.name: Literary Agent
roles.description: Strategist of campaigns — sounds the call to gather readers and convert them.

roles.name: Pamphleteer
roles.description: Carries the campaign forward into the field — content, creative, and the daily print run.

roles.name: Critic
roles.description: Records what is needed and what is promised — translates between Reader and the elegant craft of design.

roles.name: Master Novelist
roles.description: Spins the long-form work — complex platform features and the architectural prose behind the facade.

roles.name: Apprentice Scribe
roles.description: Copies fair, learns by doing, and grows into the full craft of the canon.

_realmNotes: windowOffsetWeeks values are negative so every realm starts at least one month in the past — past weeks get Time Entries (Step 6), future weeks get Schedules. Workstreams are organised as parent scope items (each with 2 child sub-scopes) — the wizard inserts parents first, then children with Parent_WorkStream__c set.

realms.projectName: 20th Century Modernists — Stream of Consciousness Platform

classical-canon.name: First Folio Compiled
classical-canon.description: Discovery sign-off — the First Folio assembled at Stationers' Hall.

classical-canon.name: Quill Cut & Inkwell Filled
classical-canon.description: Build kickoff: tools of the trade prepared for the long writing season.

classical-canon.name: Court Performance
classical-canon.description: Mid-project gate: command performance reviewed by the patron.

classical-canon.name: Patron's Stipend
classical-canon.description: Phase invoice trigger: the patron's purse opens at quarterly tithe.

classical-canon.name: Opening Night at the Globe
classical-canon.description: Go-Live: the play premieres before a full house.

classical-canon.name: Folio Bound and Shelved
classical-canon.description: Hyper-care close: the bound folio entered into the library record.

19th-century-novelists.name: Subscription Drive Opened
19th-century-novelists.description: Project kickoff: handbills posted, subscribers enrolled.

19th-century-novelists.name: First Instalment Set
19th-century-novelists.description: Build phase: the press strikes off the opening chapters.

19th-century-novelists.name: Mid-Serial Review
19th-century-novelists.description: UAT gate: critical reception of the middle instalments.

19th-century-novelists.name: Triple-Decker Published
19th-century-novelists.description: Go-Live: the three-volume novel released to the lending libraries.

19th-century-novelists.name: Author's Reading Tour
19th-century-novelists.description: Hyper-care close: the author tours the lecture circuit.

19th-century-novelists.name: Royalty Settlement
19th-century-novelists.description: Final invoice: royalties reckoned with the publisher.

20th-century-modernists.name: Manifesto Drafted
20th-century-modernists.description: Discovery: the modernist manifesto signed at the café.

20th-century-modernists.name: First Draft in Notebook
20th-century-modernists.description: Build: the long manuscript assembled longhand.

20th-century-modernists.name: Little Magazine Excerpt
20th-century-modernists.description: Solution review: pre-publication excerpt printed in The Little Review.

20th-century-modernists.name: Novel Published in Paris
20th-century-modernists.description: Go-Live: Shakespeare and Company prints the first edition.

20th-century-modernists.name: Censorship Trial Survived
20th-century-modernists.description: Project close: obscenity charges defeated; the work enters the canon.

20th-century-modernists.name: Foreign Rights Sold
20th-century-modernists.description: Final invoice: translation rights settled across Europe.

contemporary.name: Pitch Accepted by Agent
contemporary.description: Kickoff: the proposal accepted by the literary agent.

contemporary.name: Manuscript Workshopped
contemporary.description: Discovery: drafts circulated through the MFA workshop.

contemporary.name: Galleys to Reviewers
contemporary.description: Build: advance reading copies dispatched to the trade press.

contemporary.name: Booker Long-list
contemporary.description: UAT acceptance: the novel survives the long-list cull.

contemporary.name: Hardcover Release Day
contemporary.description: Go-Live + handoff: the book reaches the shelves on publication day.

contemporary.name: Royalty Statement Issued
contemporary.description: Final invoice: royalties paid out at the close of the season.

William Shakespeare.notes: Return to Stratford — domestic affairs and the second-best bed.

Charles Dickens.notes: Public reading tour — sold-out engagement at Boston.

Jane Austen.notes: Visiting at Chawton — quiet revisions to the manuscript.

Leo Tolstoy.notes: Yasnaya Polyana retreat — ploughing the fields and rethinking everything.

James Joyce.notes: Eye surgery in Zürich — convalescence required.

Virginia Woolf.notes: Monk's House, Rodmell — diary writing and quiet.

Marcel Proust.notes: Cork-lined room — strict bed rest while writing.

Gabriel García Márquez.notes: Cartagena — quiet weeks at home with Mercedes.

Margaret Atwood.notes: Pelee Island birding — annual migration count.
